cataloging_subject.label: The Babees Book and selected manners-and-meals texts (AHA6127 edited anthology)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-defined anthology slice represented by the pinned U-M/CME XML aha6127: twenty-nine selected edition-level parts resolving into thirty-seven intellectual items, with recipe clusters, counsel clusters, and parallel witness versions modeled explicitly. It is not one medieval work, one authorial collection, one manuscript, every item in Furnivall's 1868 volume, the print edition itself, the electronic encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Medieval authorship, date, place, language, form, and genre are asserted only at part level where the evidence permits.

catalog_summary.title: The Babees Book and selected manners-and-meals texts (AHA6127 edited anthology)
catalog_summary.author: Heterogeneous and principally anonymous; John Lydgate, John Russell, Peter Idley, and Symon occur only in qualified part-specific responsibilities
catalog_summary.date: Component works of varied and often uncertain medieval dates; edited anthology published 1868; U-M electronic representation dated 2006
catalog_summary.region: Varied or uncertain English textual and manuscript contexts; no single composition region assigned

titles.value: The Babees Book and selected manners-and-meals texts (AHA6127 edited anthology)
notes: The qualifier exposes the heterogeneous electronic selection boundary and prevents the anthology from being mistaken for one medieval work.

titles.value: The babees book, Aristotle's A B C, Urbanitatis, Stans puer ad mensam, The lvtille childrenes lvtil boke, The bokes of nurture of Hugh Rhodes and John Russell, Wynkyn de Worde's Boke of keruynge, The booke of demeanor, The boke of curtasye, Seager's Schoole of vertue, &c. &c. with some French and latin poems on like subjects, and some forewords on education in early England
notes: This is the U-M item title inherited from Furnivall's publication; it names material outside the selected body stream and is not a one-work title.

agents.name: John Russell
notes: The Boke of Nurture attribution is transmitted and accepted by DIMEV, while the current British Library description qualifies the Harley 4011 text as here attributed to Russell.

agents.name: Robert Grosseteste
notes: Associated with the household-statutes title, addressee, and household tradition; not assigned secure authorship here.

agents.name: Adam de Marisco
notes: Conjecturally associated with the Latin source of the household statutes; not assigned authorship of the selected Middle English translation.

agents.name: Unidentified medieval authors, translators, and compilers
Symon (unidentified transmitted signature)
notes: The transmitted signature does not securely identify a historical person or establish authorship.

scope.description: Furnivall's 1868 selection, transcription, arrangement, titles, notes, and apparatus, not medieval authorship
The heterogeneous edited anthology and its ordering

notes: The separate introductory couplet and embedded Precepts in -ly are not absorbed into Lydgate's authorship responsibility.
This preserves an ascribed/traditional authorship rather than converting the title attribution into certainty.
Possible adaptation or revision and the sixteen-line Harleian epilogue's copy-supervision context remain distinct from the principal attribution.
Only the four-line extract corresponding to Instructions lines 64–67 is assigned to Idley.
Grosseteste is an associated household authority and traditional title figure; Adam de Marisco is a conjectural Latin-source author. Neither is made the secure author of this translation.
The transmitted name is preserved without identifying the person or converting a signature into secure authorship.
This grouped responsibility records multiple anonymous creators and does not assert common authorship.

scope.description: Reliquiae Antiquae transcription used to verify the Jesus Q.G.8 Stans opening and Lansdowne 762 comparanda
Later direct edition of the Harley 279 recipe used to correct Furnivall's Harley 276 citation
Furnivall's publication-layer selection and arrangement, not one medieval composition event
The U-M electronic item represented by the pinned XML

notes: No blanket composition date is assigned to the thirty-seven heterogeneous medieval items.

scope.description: Thirty-six countable intellectual items; dialect and manuscript localization vary

language_statements.label: Latin
notes: Editorial Latin headings, rubrics, and facing material elsewhere are not counted as additional Latin intellectual items.

form_statements.description: The anthology contains twenty-five verse-only and twelve prose-only intellectual items. Parallel versions count once at family level; recipe and counsel containers are not additional intellectual items.

genre_statements.label: Edited anthology
notes: This describes the cataloging unit; no single medieval literary genre is imposed across all parts.

subject_statements.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: The selected anthology combines courtesy and conduct poems, parental instruction, dietary advice, ten culinary recipes, household statutes, short counsel texts, and several miscellaneous didactic pieces. Furnivall constructs the reading text from many manuscripts and, in three families, prints parallel witness versions. The exact selected body is therefore modeled as thirty-seven intellectual items nested under twenty-nine edition-level parts rather than as one medieval work.

The XML body has twenty-three selected non-omitted top-level divisions. Splitting each five-recipe cluster adds eight edition parts, while collapsing the two ABC divisions and the two Children's Maxims divisions into two version families subtracts two, yielding twenty-nine edition-level parts. Four zero-count containers then decompose the Dietary group and Rawlinson counsel cluster, producing thirty-seven countable intellectual items. Version children are represented but do not add intellectual items.

extent.description: Thirty-seven countable intellectual items are represented by forty-seven content-part records: twenty-one top-level manifest parts abstracting twenty-three selected top-level XML divisions, six zero-count parallel-version children, ten recipe children, three Dietary-group children, and seven Rawlinson-counsel children. The XML contains 3,603 non-note L elements. A minimal normalized selected representation is 3,607 metrical lines after resolving the Reward's two encoded prose-like L elements as four metrical lines and the Dietary introductory P as a two-line couplet. Neither 3,603 nor 3,607 is an aggregate abstract-work extent because the XML prints parallel versions of three families; aggregate verse_lines and verse_groups are therefore null.

content_parts.label: The Babees Book
content_parts.summary: A courtesy poem instructing young people of high status in virtuous behavior and table conduct.

content_parts.label: The ABC of Aristotle version family
content_parts.summary: An alphabetic conduct poem represented by two selected manuscript versions; Aristotle is invoked as title authority, not assigned as author.
notes: The two selected versions count once as an abstract work family.

content_parts.label: Lerne or be Lewde (Harley version)
content_parts.summary: The twenty-two-line Harley 5086 version.

content_parts.label: The ABC of Aristotle (complete Lambeth version)
content_parts.summary: The thirteen-line Lambeth 853 version followed in print by its alphabet list.
notes: The public LUNA image covers pp. 30–31 only; p. 32 remains outside the public selection.

content_parts.label: Urbanitatis
content_parts.summary: A conduct poem on courteous speech and behavior.
notes: Furnivall's legacy fol. 86 locator is preserved as historical evidence but is not silently equated with the current ff. 88r–v catalog locus.

content_parts.label: Children's Maxims version family
content_parts.summary: A set of behavioral maxims for children represented by two selected manuscript versions.

content_parts.label: The Lytylle Childrenes Lytil Boke
content_parts.summary: The 108-line Harley 541 version, with Egerton 1995 supplying bracketed readings and variants.

content_parts.label: The Young Children's Book
content_parts.summary: The 152-line Ashmole 61 version printed on alternating facing pages.

content_parts.label: Stans puer ad mensam version family
content_parts.summary: A table-conduct poem represented by two ninety-nine-line versions printed on alternating pages.

content_parts.label: Stans puer ad mensam (Harley 2251 / Jesus Q.G.8 selected version)
content_parts.summary: A ninety-nine-line selected version whose surviving Harley copy lacks its opening; the opening and variants are supplied through the Jesus Q.G.8 witness as printed by Wright and Halliwell.

content_parts.label: Stans puer ad mensam (Lambeth 853 version)
content_parts.summary: The ninety-nine-line Lambeth version.

content_parts.label: Of the Manners to Bring One to Honour and Welfare
content_parts.summary: Thirty-two lines of advice on manners and social conduct.

content_parts.label: Take What You Find or What You Bring
content_parts.summary: A six-line counsel text on accepting one's circumstances.

content_parts.label: The Reward of the Man Who Beggars Himself
content_parts.summary: A four-line maxim written as prose in the witness and encoded as two long L elements.
notes: The intellectual line count is four; the encoded representation has two L elements because each contains two internally rhymed metrical lines.

content_parts.label: How the Good Wife Taught Her Daughter
content_parts.summary: A maternal conduct poem counseling a daughter on domestic, religious, and social behavior.
notes: Forty-one verse lines inside editorial notes reproduce Trinity R.3.19 alternatives and are excluded from the 220-line selected base-text extent.

content_parts.label: How the Wise Man Taught His Son
content_parts.summary: A paternal instruction poem on moral and practical conduct.

content_parts.label: Recipes from Harley 5401 and corrected Fretoure source
content_parts.summary: A zero-count edition container resolved into five prose recipes; the fifth is actually from Harley 279, despite Furnivall's Harley 276 heading.

content_parts.label: Fruturs
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for a thickened apple fritter mixture.

locators.value: Furnivall page 194 / fol. 69b, retained without asserting equivalence

content_parts.label: Fruturs of Figs
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for figs fried in spiced batter.

locators.value: Furnivall page 197 / fol. 98a, retained without asserting equivalence

content_parts.label: Jussell
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for a bread-and-egg dish seasoned with sage and saffron.

locators.value: Furnivall page 198 / fol. 98b, retained without asserting equivalence

content_parts.label: Mawmeny
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for poultry in almond milk with sugar and spices.

locators.value: Furnivall page 201 / fol. 100a, retained without asserting equivalence

content_parts.label: Fretoure
content_parts.summary: A prose apple-fritter recipe whose Furnivall heading misidentifies Harley 276; direct comparison and Austin's edition locate it in Harley 279.
notes: Harley 276 is retained only as evidence of Furnivall's erroneous citation and is never modeled as the target witness.

content_parts.label: A Diatorie textual group
content_parts.summary: A zero-count Lambeth 853 container comprising an anonymous introductory couplet, Lydgate's incomplete and disarranged Dietary witness, and the separately indexed Precepts in -ly embedded at its end.

locators.value: 54, 56, 58; facing Latin on 55, 57 omitted from the selected intellectual count

notes: DIMEV 2369's p. 182–185 witness range contains DIMEV 4810 at p. 185 and must not be summed with it as a disjoint physical span.

content_parts.label: To be ruled by this dietary do thy diligence
content_parts.summary: An anonymous rubric-like introductory couplet unique to the Lambeth presentation of Lydgate's Dietary.
notes: Modeled as a full two-line work because DIMEV gives it its own bibliographic record, while preserving its functionally paratextual placement and only-witness ambiguity.

content_parts.label: John Lydgate's Dietary
content_parts.summary: The Lambeth witness preserves eight of the poem's ten proper stanzas in disarranged order; stanzas 4 and 7 are wanting.
notes: The selected stanza order is 2, 1, 8, 9, 3, 6, 5, 10 relative to the proper sequence.

content_parts.label: Serve thy God truly (Precepts in -ly)
content_parts.summary: An anonymous eight-line precept poem incorporated at the end of the Lambeth Dietary witness but independently indexed and widely witnessed.

content_parts.label: Recipes from Harley 279
content_parts.summary: A zero-count edition container resolved into five prose recipes from Harley 279.

content_parts.label: Harys in Cyueye
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for hare in onion sauce.

content_parts.label: Conyngys in Cyveye
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for rabbit in sauce.

content_parts.label: Doucettes (cream filling)
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for cream-and-egg custard pies.

content_parts.label: Doucettes (pork filling)
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for pork-and-egg pies.

content_parts.label: Doucettes a-forcyd
content_parts.summary: A prose recipe for fortified almond-milk custard pies.

content_parts.label: John Russell's Boke of Nurture
content_parts.summary: An extensive household and service poem on duties, meals, carving, and elite conduct.
notes: Twelve editorial-comparanda verse lines inside notes are excluded from the 1,252-line selected text.

content_parts.label: The Boke of Curtasye
content_parts.summary: A long courtesy poem treating social, household, and table conduct.

content_parts.label: Household Statutes associated with Bishop Grosseteste
content_parts.summary: A Middle English prose translation of household regulations traditionally associated with Robert Grosseteste's episcopal household.
notes: The title association, addressee, translator, and conjectural Latin-source author are separate responsibility layers.

content_parts.label: Stanzas and Couplets of Counsel from Rawlinson C.86
content_parts.summary: A zero-count physical and editorial cluster of seven separately cataloged verse items on one manuscript page.
notes: DIMEV 6137's legacy group description is preserved, but the current Bodleian catalog's seven nested msItems govern the decomposition.

content_parts.label: Utter thy language with good advisement
content_parts.summary: An anonymous seven-line rhyme-royal stanza on prudent speech and friendship.

content_parts.label: What man thou servest, always him dread
content_parts.summary: Four lines corresponding to lines 64–67 of Peter Idley's Instructions.

content_parts.label: Fools lade pools; wise men eat the fish
content_parts.summary: An anonymous two-line aphorism contrasting fools and wise people.

content_parts.label: Who in youth no virtue uses
content_parts.summary: An anonymous two-line proverb linking youthful virtue to honor in age.

content_parts.label: Deem the best in every doubt
content_parts.summary: An anonymous two-line moralizing proverb counseling charitable judgment.

content_parts.label: It is the property of a gentleman
content_parts.summary: An anonymous two-line proverbial statement about a gentleman's speech.

content_parts.label: Si vis dolere tua crimina dic miserere
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Latin penitential couplet cataloged as Walter 18075.

content_parts.label: Whatever Thou Say, Advise Thee Well
content_parts.summary: A seventy-two-line counsel poem on prudent speech and behavior.

content_parts.label: A Dogg Lardyner, and a Sowe Gardyner
content_parts.summary: A brief prose saying about unsuitable household officers.

content_parts.label: Maxims in -ly
content_parts.summary: A sixteen-line sequence of concise behavioral maxims.

content_parts.label: Symon's Lesson of Wisdom for All Manner Children
content_parts.summary: A 102-line didactic poem for children, transmitted with the name Symon but without a securely identified author.

related_works.title: Peter Idley's Instructions
notes: The selected Rawlinson four-line item corresponds to Instructions lines 64–67; the anthology does not contain the complete work.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha6127.xml, pinned to CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for electronic identity, title, imprint, selected and omitted body divisions, headings, text, print-page and manuscript locators, verse structures, notes, and the 2006 OCR/encoding declaration.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of the pinned aha6127 XML.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1, nested DIV2, LG, L, P, NOTE, NOTE1, NOTE2, PB, and MILESTONE counts
evidence.summary: Reproducibly establishes twenty-three selected non-omitted top-level divisions, twenty-nine edition-level parts, thirty-seven intellectual items, 3,656 raw L elements, fifty-three L elements inside notes, 3,603 non-note L elements, and the minimally normalized 3,607-line representation. It also establishes why aggregate abstract-work verse metrics must remain null in the presence of parallel versions.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Babees Book and related texts, EETS Original Series 32 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868).
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for edited-selection boundaries, titles, sequence, print and witness locators, alternating versions, recipes, apparatus, responsibility statements, and the edition's Harley 276 error.

evidence.citation: Thomas Austin, ed., Two Fifteenth-Century Cookery-Books (London: N. Trübner for EETS, 1888).
evidence.locator: printed p. 44; Harley 279 Vyaunde Leche no. 54
evidence.summary: Direct later edition confirming the Fretoure text in Harley 279 and thereby refuting Furnivall's Harley 276 heading.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, eds., Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 1 (London, 1845).
evidence.summary: Historical edition evidence for the Jesus College Stans opening and variants and for the Lansdowne Dogg Lardyner and Maxims comparison texts.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 data release, records 5964.5, 2369, 4810, 6137, 6242, 1333, 1867, 1112, and 2745.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-5964.5, record-2369, record-4810, record-6137, record-6242, record-1333, record-1867, record-1112, record-2745; exact witness IDs stated in lineage
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly evidence for the Dietary-group decomposition, independent Precepts record, Rawlinson counsel identities, authorship, forms, line counts, exact witnesses, and group-versus-work caveats.

evidence.citation: Current official manuscript catalog records and digital-surrogate inventories modeled in the aha6127 lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence for current shelfmarks, precise manuscript loci, physical and digital availability, the Urbanitatis foliation discrepancy, Harley 276 refutation, Lambeth page contents, Rawlinson seven-item decomposition, Jesus Q.G.8/Q.T.8 equivalence, and the status of public facsimiles.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for aha6127.
evidence.summary: Separates the edited anthology, electronic encoding, repository artifact, scholarly editions, witness sources, public and requestable surrogates, access routes, local cache inventory, and rights layers.

open_questions.question: Should DIMEV 5964.5 ultimately remain a countable anonymous work or be reclassified as witness-specific paratext to Lydgate's Dietary?
next_steps: Compare every known witness and full DIMEV editorial documentation.
Retain the current countable-work decision while explicitly preserving its rubric-like function and only-witness status.

open_questions.question: Should any of the seven Rawlinson f. 31r items be grouped as one historical compilation rather than seven intellectual items?
next_steps: Preserve DIMEV 6137's legacy aggregate description as evidence.
Use the current Bodleian seven-msItem decomposition unless stronger work-level evidence appears.

open_questions.question: Can complete authorized surrogates of every nonpublic principal witness be obtained with clear reuse terms?
next_steps: Plan the whole-object-first institutional enquiry routes recorded in lineage; no request has yet been submitted.
If enquiries are later sent, record image supply, reuse permission, and contract terms as separate response layers.

notes: The cataloging subject is an edited selective anthology, not a single medieval work.
The 3,603 and 3,607 figures describe encoded or minimally normalized selected representations only; neither is stored as aggregate verse extent.
No blanket author, medieval date, composition region, or literary genre is asserted across all parts.
Transmission layers, physical witnesses, catalog descriptions, digital surrogates, access, and rights are modeled in the linked lineage manifest.
